fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that compile-time sequence toolkit we've been talking about. Basically it's a C++ metaprogramming library thing — the core idea is that developers should be able to describe lists of types or numbers at compile time and do stuff with them (inspect, transform, search, sort, etc.) without writing tons of boilerplate. The adapter layer should read JSON commands from stdin and spit out results to stdout, similar to how we handled the dispatch logic in that type-registry module we built before.

The tricky parts are around how sequences get printed — there's a specific bracket format we need to follow, and the search feature has some nuance around what counts as a 'match' depending on whether you're working with types or numbers. Also the reduction feature has this weird order-sensitive rule that someone on the backend team spec'd out but I don't think it's written down anywhere obvious.

We also need a test harness script that runs all the JSON case files and saves raw output per case to a specific folder structure. Output files should never mix results from different case directories. The whole thing should be organized sensibly — don't just dump everything in one file but also don't over-engineer it. Let me know if anything's unclear, I'll try to dig up the old notes.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: int main() // adapter reads JSON from stdin, dispatches to core logic, writes to stdout

trigger_keywords: how should sequences be printed
output format for lists
bracket notation for sequence results
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Sequences must be printed using square bracket notation with comma-separated values and no spaces, e.g. `sequence=[0,1,2]`. Single observations like size, front, back, found, result are printed as `key=value` on individual lines. Each line ends with a newline character.

trigger_keywords: what counts as a match in search
floating point predicate
nonzero match rule for value sequences
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For type sequences, the match predicate targets floating-point types specifically (e.g. float, double). For value sequences, zero is treated as non-matching and any non-zero value is treated as matching. If no match is found, `found=no` is printed and the returned sequence is empty `[]`.

trigger_keywords: order-sensitive reduction rule
add odd multiply even
the special fold operation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The order-sensitive reduction rule (sometimes called 'odd_even' or similar) processes elements by position: elements at odd positions (1-indexed) are added to the accumulator, and elements at even positions multiply the accumulator. This is applied left-to-right starting from the initial value provided.

trigger_keywords: reverse range generation
countdown direction
how does reverse range work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Reverse range generation counts down starting from `start` (inclusive) and excludes `stop`, producing values in descending order. Equal start and stop produce an empty sequence. For example, start=3, stop=0 produces [3,2,1].

trigger_keywords: test harness output folder structure
where are test outputs saved
stdout file naming convention
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness writes one file per case to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/`. The filename format is `{feature_file_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt`, e.g. `feature1_type_sequence_access@000.txt`. The `--cases-dir` flag defaults to `public_test_cases`. Different case directories never share output folders, preventing overwrites.

trigger_keywords: idempotent insertion for keyed association
duplicate key behavior
what happens if you insert an existing key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For keyed type association (Feature 9), inserting a key that already exists leaves the association set unchanged — the existing mapping is preserved and no duplicate is added. For unique type membership (Feature 10), inserting a type already in the set also leaves it unchanged; inserting a new type appends it at the end.

trigger_keywords: type size ordering
how are types sorted by size
which platform sizes are used for type sort
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Type sequences ordered by size use the target platform's ordinary scalar type sizes as determined by the test adapter's compiled environment (i.e. `sizeof()` values on the build machine). Types are ordered smallest to largest. The adapter is responsible for mapping type name strings to their sizes at compile or run time.

trigger_keywords: add_pointer transformation format
how is pointer type rendered
void star pointer output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `add_pointer` transformation appends a `*` directly to the type name string with no space. For example, `float` becomes `float*`, and `void*` becomes `void**`. This applies uniformly to all types in the sequence.

trigger_keywords: zip or pair transformation
pairing two sequences
how does the zip transform output look
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The pairing transformation (zip) takes two same-length type sequences and produces a sequence of pairs rendered as `type1:type2` per element, enclosed in the standard bracket notation, e.g. `sequence=[int:bool,char:float]`.

trigger_keywords: file structure requirements
single file or multi file
how should the project be organized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must NOT be a single god file. Given the multiple distinct responsibilities (type metaprogramming core, value metaprogramming core, adapter/dispatcher, test harness), it should use a multi-file layout with clear separation: a `src/` or equivalent area for core headers/logic, a separate adapter entry point, and `rcb_tests/` for the test harness. The core domain must be decoupled from JSON parsing and stdout I/O.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the dispatch logic in that type-registry module we built before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON adapter/dispatcher pattern described in the PRD's execution adapter requirement: reads a JSON command from stdin, inspects the `task` field, and routes to the appropriate core domain function, then prints formatted output to stdout

context_pointers.vague_reference: this weird order-sensitive rule that someone on the backend team spec'd out but I don't think it's written down anywhere obvious
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The reduction rule in Feature 7 where odd-indexed elements are added and even-indexed elements multiply the accumulator, referenced in `feature7_reduce_sequences.json` as one of the supported `reduction` values

context_pointers.vague_reference: output files should never mix results from different case directories
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/` namespacing requirement in the test harness spec: the `--cases-dir` argument value is used as a subdirectory so runs with different case directories produce isolated output trees

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the exact spec detail handy. I'd suggest checking the `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` JSON files for the relevant feature — the expected_output fields there are the ground truth for formatting. If it's about architecture or dispatch logic, the start.md file has the authoritative constraints.

feature10_unique_type_membership.json.description: Represents a compile-time set of unique types and supports membership queries, insertion, and removal.

feature1_type_sequence_access.json.description: Observes the size and boundary elements of a sequence of types without constructing runtime objects.

feature2_value_sequence_access.json.description: Observes the size, boundary values, indexed values, and concatenation behavior of a sequence of integral constants.

feature3_range_generation.json.description: Generates forward half-open integer ranges and reverse countdown ranges as value sequences.

feature4_iterative_sequence_generation.j.description: Generates a fixed number of values by repeatedly applying a one-step increment or decrement operation from a starting value.

feature5_transform_sequences.json.description: Transforms each element of one or two input sequences while preserving order and sequence length.

feature6_search_sequences.json.description: Searches type and value sequences and returns the suffix or prefix located at the first or last matching element.

feature7_reduce_sequences.json.description: Reduces a sequence from left to right using a supplied initial value and operation.

feature8_sort_sequences.json.description: Orders value sequences by numeric comparison and type sequences by a caller-provided type-size ordering.

feature9_keyed_type_association.json.description: Stores compile-time associations from key types to value types and supports lookup plus idempotent insertion.
